title: Ultrafast electron dynamics of electron-irradiated graphene
abstract: Electron irradiation is essential for materials characterization and modification, though the fundamental interactions between incident electrons and host materials remain under investigation. Here, we employ first-principles simulations to study electron dynamics under external electron irradiation. We quantify differences in key observables, including kinetic energy loss, secondary electron emission, and backscattered electrons, between classical and quantum mechanical descriptions of the incident electron. Around 400 eV incident energy, we identify significant differences in backscattered electron yields between classical point-charge and quantum wave-packet descriptions, whereas the quantum-mechanical effects diminish at incident energies above 600 eV. These differences highlight the critical importance of quantum effects in electron irradiation phenomena that occur in a specific energy range of the incident electron. Our results provide clear guidance for selecting appropriate incident, electron descriptions based on kinetic-energy regimes, identify a targeted experimental window for isolating quantum-only backscattering, and enable the rational design of 2D materials for nanofabrication and high-resolution electron-beam technologies.
summary: This study uses first-principles simulations to show that classical models are insufficient for describing electron irradiation of graphene at energies around 400 eV. It identifies a specific energy regime where quantum wave-packet effects significantly alter backscattering yields. These findings provide guidance for using electron beams in high-resolution microscopy and nanofabrication.
main_problem: Investigating the fundamental interactions between incident electrons and graphene during irradiation, specifically determining when classical point-charge descriptions fail and quantum mechanical wave-packet effects become necessary.
main_result: Identified a specific energy window around 400 eV where quantum effects like diffraction and reflection lead to a finite backscattered electron yield, whereas classical descriptions predict zero backscattering.
method: Real-time time-dependent density functional theory (rt-TDDFT) using Ehrenfest dynamics and the INQ code.
model_or_system: A single-layer graphene sheet (112 carbon atoms) subjected to incident electrons modeled either as a Gaussian quantum wave packet or a classical ensemble of point-charge trajectories.
key_observables: Kinetic energy loss (electronic stopping), secondary electron emission (SEE) yield, and backscattered electron yield/momentum distribution.
important_parameters: Incident kinetic energy (100 eV to 800 eV), de Broglie wavelength, and wave packet width.
main_assumptions: Use of the adiabatic local density approximation (ALDA) for exchange-correlation and Gamma-point Brillouin zone sampling.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the simulation cell and electron density change; Figure 2 compares momentum distributions for different electron models; Figure 3 decomposes kinetic energy loss; Figure 4 shows secondary electron yields; Figure 5 and 6 show backscattering trends and kinetic energy spectra.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of electron-material interaction, describes the rt-TDDFT methodology and validation, presents results comparing classical vs. quantum dynamics across energy regimes, and concludes with implications for materials design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. A schematic of the simulation cell and the planar integrated charge density difference after the electron pro- jectile impacts the graphene (dark green circles represent C atoms) at 0.28 fs after the simulation starts. The initial wave packet is located at ⟨z⟩= −20 a0 and approaches the graphene perpendicularly. The solid green line denotes the region of the complex absorbing potential, Eq. S2. Two traces of the wave packet are visible, before and after encountering the graphene layer, since this plot shows the electron density difference.

Figure 2. Comparison of the momentum distribution of electrons as a function of the mean position ⟨z⟩for the wave packet (WP) versus classical approaches with the mean veloc- ity corresponding to 300 eV. The WP and ensemble of clas- sical trajectories are centered at the channeling trajectory, Traj. A. We also include one additional centroid trajectory, Traj. O. The solid lines and shaded regions denote the mean and the standard deviation of the momentum, respectively. The impact point of the Traj. A and Traj. O is marked in Fig. S2.

Figure 3. The kinetic energy loss of electrons described as a quantum mechanical wave packet (WP), an ensemble of classical electrons, or a single classical electron, centered on two distinct trajectories: channeling (Traj. A) and centroid (Traj. O). The total kinetic energy loss, ∆Ekin, calculated from Eq. (4) and plotted in panel (a), is decomposed into (b) contributions from the average momentum, ∆ � ⟨p⟩2 /2  , and (c) the momentum spreading, ∆ � σ2 p/2  . The error bar in the classical ensemble indicates the standard error of the mean of the kinetic energy loss.

Figure 4. Secondary electron emission at (a) the entrance side and (b) the exit side of graphene irradiated by: a wave packet (WP) centered at the channeling trajectory (Traj. A), a sin- gle classical electron along the channeling trajectory (Traj. A), and a single classical electron along the centroid trajec- tory (Traj. O). The solid line represents the universal law for secondary electron yield derived by Lin et al. in Ref. [55], see Sec. S6 in the SI for details of how we calculate this curve.

Figure 5. The ratio of the backscattered electrons to the total incident electrons simulated from the wave packet (WP), and an ensemble of classical trajectories, compared with the ex- perimental backscattered yield for a bulk graphite [62], shifted down by a constant 0.15. The vertical black dashed line sep- arates where the quantum mechanical effect vanishes. The region where the classical effect vanishes and the purely quan- tum regime appears is shaded in purple.
